findings.summary: Five observe/verification.rs endpoints have no authorization checks: run_verification, run_simulation, handle_design_time_stream, handle_workflows, and get_paths. These handlers do not accept HeaderMap and never call require_observe_auth, while all sibling observe endpoints consistently enforce auth.
evidence: run_verification (line 29) takes only State and Path
run_simulation (line 232) takes State, Path, Query
handle_design_time_stream (line 399) takes only State
handle_workflows (line 459) takes only State
get_paths (line 731) takes State, Path, Query
None of these endpoints accept HeaderMap or call require_observe_auth
Compare with handle_verification_status (line 310) which correctly calls require_observe_auth and observe_tenant_scope
findings.suggestion: Add HeaderMap parameter and require_observe_auth call to all five endpoints. For run_verification use action 'run_verification', for run_simulation/get_paths use 'read_verification', for handle_design_time_stream/handle_workflows use 'read_events'. Also add observe_tenant_scope to handle_workflows to filter by tenant.

findings.summary: POST /api/evolution/trajectories/unmet (handle_unmet_intent in trajectories.rs) has no authorization check. It accepts arbitrary JSON from callers and persists trajectory entries without validating identity.
evidence: handle_unmet_intent (line 91) takes State and Json(body) but no HeaderMap
No call to require_observe_auth or any other authorization check
It directly persists trajectory data and runs sentinel analysis
findings.suggestion: Add HeaderMap and require_observe_auth with action 'write_trajectories' on resource 'Trajectory'. Consider also validating the tenant field from the body against the caller's tenant scope.

findings.summary: agents.rs now consistently uses both require_observe_auth and observe_tenant_scope, matching the pattern established in entities.rs, specs.rs, and wasm.rs. This is a well-applied authorization pattern across the observe module's data-reading endpoints.
evidence: agents.rs list_agents (line 88-89) and get_agent_history (line 139-140) both call require_observe_auth then observe_tenant_scope
entities.rs list_entities (line 27-28), get_entity_history (line 70), and handle_event_stream (line 186-187) follow the same pattern
specs.rs list_specs (line 26-27) and get_spec_detail (line 87-88) also match
This is consistent and well-factored
findings.suggestion: No change needed for these endpoints. This pattern should be applied to the verification.rs endpoints identified above.

findings.summary: Several Turso tables lack row-level security tenant scoping: evolution_records, feature_requests, pending_decisions, and tenant_policies. While these tables have tenant columns with indexes, queries against them do not consistently enforce tenant filtering at the data access layer.
evidence: schema.rs shows evolution_records (line 202), feature_requests (line 188), pending_decisions (line 133), and tenant_policies (line 152) have tenant columns but are listed in the blind packet as without_rls
The observe endpoints use require_observe_auth but some evolution queries (list_evolution_records, list_evolution_insights) do not apply observe_tenant_scope to filter results to the caller's tenant
findings.suggestion: Add observe_tenant_scope calls to the evolution record listing endpoints and pass tenant_filter to the Turso query methods. This ensures non-admin callers only see records from their own tenant.

findings.summary: No integration tests verify that authorization is enforced on observe endpoints. The mod_test.rs file uses system_get() helper which bypasses auth checks, but there are no tests that verify unauthorized requests are rejected with 403.
evidence: mod_test.rs at 1221 LOC tests the happy path of observe endpoints using system_get() which sets X-Temper-Principal-Kind: System, always passing require_observe_auth
No test sends requests without auth headers to verify 403 responses
The server integration tests (dst_lifecycle, multi_tenant, etc.) do not test observe endpoint authorization either
findings.suggestion: Add tests that send requests without X-Temper-Principal-Kind header (or with kind=User without tenant) to verify require_observe_auth returns 403. Cover at least list_specs, list_entities, list_agents, and list_evolution_records.

findings.summary: Several high-complexity production modules have zero test coverage: query_eval.rs (746 LOC, complexity 341), entity_ops.rs (579 LOC), cascade.rs (630 LOC), and parser.rs (731 LOC). These are critical path modules for OData query evaluation, entity state operations, verification cascade, and spec parsing.
evidence: The blind packet marks these as 'Untested module' with 0 importers (orphaned files)
query_eval.rs has complexity score 341 and handles OData filter/orderby/select evaluation -- a critical data path
entity_ops.rs at 579 LOC handles entity creation, deletion, and state dispatch
cascade.rs at 630 LOC orchestrates the L0-L3 verification pipeline
findings.suggestion: Prioritize unit tests for query_eval.rs filter evaluation logic and entity_ops.rs dispatch paths. These are high-value targets because errors in query evaluation silently return wrong data, and entity_ops errors affect all entity state transitions.

findings.summary: The observe module's test file mod_test.rs at 1221 LOC contains 37 functions across 6 disconnected clusters, mixing spec listing tests, entity history tests, trajectory tests, evolution record tests, workflow tests, and insight tests in one file.
evidence: Blind packet reports: '6 disconnected function clusters (37 functions) -- likely mixed responsibilities'
The file tests specs, entities, trajectories, evolution records, workflows, and insights all in one 1221 LOC file
This makes it difficult to identify coverage gaps for specific observe sub-modules
findings.suggestion: Split mod_test.rs into per-module test files (specs_test.rs, entities_test.rs, evolution_test.rs, etc.) to improve navigability and make coverage gaps in specific sub-modules visible.

findings.summary: temper-server/src/ root contains 13 files with mixed concerns: routing (router.rs), query evaluation (query_eval.rs at 746 LOC), event sourcing (event_store.rs, events.rs), idempotency (idempotency.rs), registry bootstrap (registry_bootstrap.rs at 11.8KB), sentinel (sentinel.rs at 10.1KB), and WASM registry (wasm_registry.rs). Several of these share affinity with existing subdirectories.
evidence: The directory profile shows 13 files with 3301 total LOC at the root level
query_eval.rs (746 LOC) is an OData query evaluator that belongs in odata/
sentinel.rs (10KB) implements evolution sentinel rules and belongs in observe/evolution/
registry_bootstrap.rs (11.8KB) handles spec loading into the SpecRegistry and belongs in registry/
wasm_registry.rs handles WASM module metadata and could move under state/dispatch/ or a dedicated wasm/ module
findings.suggestion: Move query_eval.rs into odata/ as odata/query_eval.rs. Move sentinel.rs into observe/evolution/sentinel.rs. Move registry_bootstrap.rs into registry/bootstrap.rs. Consider grouping event_store.rs and events.rs under an events/ subdirectory.

findings.summary: The observe/ subdirectory is well-organized with clear separation: agents.rs, entities.rs, metrics.rs, specs.rs, verification.rs, wasm.rs, and evolution/ as a sub-module. Each file owns a distinct endpoint group with consistent naming. The evolution/ sub-module further separates operations, records_detail, records_list, and trajectories.
evidence: observe/ has 10 files at the top level and 5 files in the evolution/ subdirectory, each covering a distinct endpoint group
mod.rs cleanly defines the router and shared types
The specs/ subdirectory further separates load_dir, load_inline, types, and verification_stream
Total LOC across observe/ is 3510 + 1594 = 5104, distributed across 15 files averaging 340 LOC per file
findings.suggestion: No structural changes needed. Consider extracting verification.rs (776 LOC) into a verification/ subdirectory if it continues to grow.

findings.summary: Multiple production files exceed the project's 500 LOC threshold: entity_actor/actor.rs (839), observe/verification.rs (776), query_eval.rs (746), observe/mod_test.rs (1221), evolution/records.rs (655), store-redis/event_store.rs (578), entity_ops.rs (579), and several others. The project guide mandates splitting files over 500 LOC into directory modules.
evidence: CLAUDE.md states 'Files > 500 lines must be split into directory modules'
entity_actor/actor.rs is at 839 LOC with complexity score 528 -- the highest in the project

verification.rs at 776 LOC handles verification cascade execution, simulation, verification-status, design-time SSE stream, workflows, and path extraction -- at least 6 distinct responsibilities in one file
query_eval.rs at 746 LOC has complexity score 341
findings.suggestion: Split verification.rs into verification/ with cascade.rs, simulation.rs, status.rs, stream.rs, and workflow.rs. Split entity_actor/actor.rs by extracting persistence logic and transition execution into separate files. Split query_eval.rs by separating filter evaluation from select/orderby/expand logic.

findings.summary: record_authz_denial in authz/helpers.rs has 11 parameters (with #[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]) and performs 5 distinct responsibilities: building a PendingDecision, broadcasting via SSE, persisting to Turso, creating a GovernanceDecision system entity, and recording a trajectory entry.
evidence: Function signature at line 128 takes state, tenant, security_ctx, agent_id_override, action, resource_type, resource_id, resource_attrs, reason, module_name, and from_status
The function body (lines 128-222, 94 lines) performs SSE broadcast (line 161)
Turso decision persistence (line 164)
System entity dispatch (lines 181-196)
Trajectory persistence (lines 198-219)
findings.suggestion: Introduce a DenialRecordInput struct to bundle the 11 parameters. Consider extracting the system entity creation and trajectory recording into separate helper functions that record_authz_denial calls, reducing its responsibility to orchestration only.

findings.summary: wide_event.rs now uses typed input structs (TransitionInput, WasmInvocationInput, AuthzDecisionInput, InvariantCheckInput) for its builder functions instead of long parameter lists. This is a well-applied pattern that makes the API self-documenting and prevents parameter ordering errors.
evidence: TransitionInput (lines 107-128) groups 11 fields with doc comments
from_transition (line 131) takes a single TransitionInput reference
WasmInvocationInput (lines 170-187) groups 8 fields
AuthzDecisionInput (lines 228-241) groups 6 fields
InvariantCheckInput (lines 278-293) groups 7 fields
Each has comprehensive tests (lines 398-600)
findings.suggestion: This pattern should be adopted by record_authz_denial and dispatch/wasm.rs functions that currently suppress too_many_arguments.

findings.summary: dispatch/wasm.rs has 5 functions suppressing clippy::too_many_arguments, indicating systemic parameter proliferation in the WASM dispatch path. Functions dispatch_single_integration, handle_module_not_found, invoke_and_handle_result, record_invocation, and handle_wasm_failure all have wide parameter lists.
evidence: 5 instances of #[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)] at lines 75, 180, 222, 331, and 372
This is the highest concentration of parameter-count suppressions in the codebase (total of 15 across 10 files)
All functions in this file deal with the same entity reference context (tenant, entity_type, entity_id, action, module_name) that could be bundled
findings.suggestion: Introduce a WasmDispatchContext struct bundling the shared parameters (entity_ref already exists as WasmEntityRef but is not used for all shared parameters). Adopt the input struct pattern from wide_event.rs across these functions.

findings.summary: The observe authorization API surface is well-designed: require_observe_auth takes (state, headers, action, resource_type) consistently, and observe_tenant_scope takes (state, headers) consistently. All callers follow the same two-step pattern.
evidence: require_observe_auth is called 17 times across observe modules with consistent 4-parameter shape
observe_tenant_scope is called 8 times with consistent 2-parameter shape

The action strings follow a naming convention: read_agents, read_entities, read_events, read_specs, read_verification, read_wasm, read_evolution, read_trajectories, run_sentinel, manage_feature_requests, manage_wasm
findings.suggestion: Document the action/resource_type vocabulary as constants in the authz module to prevent typos and make the permission model discoverable.

findings.summary: Observe endpoints use inconsistent error return types: some return Result<Json<T>, StatusCode>, others return Result<Json<T>, (StatusCode, String)>. This affects the API consumer's ability to get error details.
evidence: agents.rs list_agents returns Result<Json<Value>, StatusCode> (bare status code, no message)
wasm.rs upload_wasm_module returns Result<Json<T>, (StatusCode, String)> (status code with message)
verification.rs run_verification returns Result<Json<T>, StatusCode>
This means some error responses include a human-readable reason while others do not
findings.suggestion: Standardize on Result<Json<T>, (StatusCode, String)> for all endpoints that can fail with actionable error messages. Alternatively, introduce a shared ApiError type that implements IntoResponse with consistent JSON error bodies.

findings.summary: wide_event.rs has thorough test coverage with 12 test functions covering all 4 event kinds (Transition, WasmInvocation, AuthzDecision, InvariantCheck), success/failure paths, cost decoupling assertions, and view projection smoke tests.
evidence: Tests span lines 398-600 (200 lines, 33% of file)
test_wide_event_from_transition verifies tags, attributes, and measurements
test_cost_decoupling asserts entity_id is NOT in tags (only in attributes)
test_wasm_invocation_event, test_authz_decision_event, test_invariant_check_event cover all EventKind variants
test_emit_span_all_event_kinds exercises all 4 kinds through both emit_span and emit_metrics
findings.suggestion: No changes needed. This is a good model for test coverage in other modules.
